question:
Show that if the points of an isosceles right triangle of side length
1 are each colored with one of four colors, then there must be two points
of the same color whch are at least a distance $2 - \sqrt{2}$ apart.
solution:
Solution. Suppose that it is possible to color the points of the triangle with four colors so that any two points at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \) apart receive different colors. Suppose the vertices of the triangle are \( A=(0,0), B=(1,0), C=(0,1) \). Define also the points \( D=(\sqrt{2}-1,0) \) and \( E=(0, \sqrt{2}-1) \) on the two sides, and \( F=(2-\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}-1) \) and \( G=(\sqrt{2}-1,2-\sqrt{2}) \) on the diagonal; see Figure 27. Note that
\[
B D=B F=C E=C G=D E=D G=E F=2-\sqrt{2}
\]

The color of \( B \) must be different from the colors of the other named points. The same holds for \( C \). Thus the vertices of the pentagram \( A G D E F \) must each be painted one of the two remaining colors. Then two adjacent vertices of the pentagram must have the same color. But they are separated by at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \), so this is a contradiction.

descriptive_long.question:
Show that if the points of an isosceles right triangle of side length
1 are each colored with one of four colors, then there must be two points
of the same color whch are at least a distance $2 - \sqrt{2}$ apart.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose that it is possible to color the points of the triangle with four colors so that any two points at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \) apart receive different colors. Suppose the vertices of the triangle are \( vertexa=(0,0), vertexb=(1,0), vertexc=(0,1) \). Define also the points \( vertexd=(\sqrt{2}-1,0) \) and \( vertexe=(0, \sqrt{2}-1) \) on the two sides, and \( vertexf=(2-\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}-1) \) and \( vertexg=(\sqrt{2}-1,2-\sqrt{2}) \) on the diagonal; see Figure 27. Note that
\[
vertexb vertexd=vertexb vertexf=vertexc vertexe=vertexc vertexg=vertexd vertexe=vertexd vertexg=vertexe vertexf=2-\sqrt{2}
\]

The color of \( vertexb \) must be different from the colors of the other named points. The same holds for \( vertexc \). Thus the vertices of the pentagram \( vertexa vertexg vertexd vertexe vertexf \) must each be painted one of the two remaining colors. Then two adjacent vertices of the pentagram must have the same color. But they are separated by at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \), so this is a contradiction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Show that if the points of an isosceles right triangle of side length
1 are each colored with one of four colors, then there must be two points
of the same color whch are at least a distance $2 - \sqrt{2}$ apart.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose that it is possible to color the points of the triangle with four colors so that any two points at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \) apart receive different colors. Suppose the vertices of the triangle are \( blueberry=(0,0), pinecone=(1,0), rainstorm=(0,1) \). Define also the points \( horseshoe=(\sqrt{2}-1,0) \) and \( goldfish=(0, \sqrt{2}-1) \) on the two sides, and \( thumbtack=(2-\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}-1) \) and \( paintbrush=(\sqrt{2}-1,2-\sqrt{2}) \) on the diagonal; see Figure 27. Note that
\[
pinecone\ horseshoe = pinecone\ thumbtack = rainstorm\ goldfish = rainstorm\ paintbrush = horseshoe\ goldfish = horseshoe\ paintbrush = goldfish\ thumbtack = 2-\sqrt{2}
\]

The color of \( pinecone \) must be different from the colors of the other named points. The same holds for \( rainstorm \). Thus the vertices of the pentagram \( blueberry\ paintbrush\ horseshoe\ goldfish\ thumbtack \) must each be painted one of the two remaining colors. Then two adjacent vertices of the pentagram must have the same color. But they are separated by at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \), so this is a contradiction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Show that if the points of an isosceles right triangle of side length
1 are each colored with one of four colors, then there must be two points
of the same color whch are at least a distance $2 - \sqrt{2}$ apart.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Suppose that it is possible to color the points of the triangle with four colors so that any two points at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \) apart receive different colors. Suppose the vertices of the triangle are \( emptiness=(0,0), voidpoint=(1,0), nullspot=(0,1) \). Define also the points \( blanknode=(\sqrt{2}-1,0) \) and \( hollowplace=(0, \sqrt{2}-1) \) on the two sides, and \( scarcity=(2-\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}-1) \) and \( nonentity=(\sqrt{2}-1,2-\sqrt{2}) \) on the diagonal; see Figure 27. Note that
\[
voidpoint blanknode = voidpoint scarcity = nullspot hollowplace = nullspot nonentity = blanknode hollowplace = blanknode nonentity = hollowplace scarcity = 2-\sqrt{2}
\]

The color of \( voidpoint \) must be different from the colors of the other named points. The same holds for \( nullspot \). Thus the vertices of the pentagram \( emptiness nonentity blanknode hollowplace scarcity \) must each be painted one of the two remaining colors. Then two adjacent vertices of the pentagram must have the same color. But they are separated by at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \), so this is a contradiction.

garbled_string.question:
Show that if the points of an isosceles right triangle of side length
1 are each colored with one of four colors, then there must be two points
of the same color whch are at least a distance $2 - \sqrt{2}$ apart.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Suppose that it is possible to color the points of the triangle with four colors so that any two points at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \) apart receive different colors. Suppose the vertices of the triangle are \( xqvmsldp=(0,0), hregtcoa=(1,0), pqzwkfnb=(0,1) \). Define also the points \( myjvnxsr=(\sqrt{2}-1,0) \) and \( kuladqwe=(0, \sqrt{2}-1) \) on the two sides, and \( zasbqmnr=(2-\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}-1) \) and \( vlochwer=(\sqrt{2}-1,2-\sqrt{2}) \) on the diagonal; see Figure 27. Note that
\[
hregtcoa\ myjvnxsr = hregtcoa\ zasbqmnr = pqzwkfnb\ kuladqwe = pqzwkfnb\ vlochwer = myjvnxsr\ kuladqwe = myjvnxsr\ vlochwer = kuladqwe\ zasbqmnr = 2-\sqrt{2}
\]

The color of \( hregtcoa \) must be different from the colors of the other named points. The same holds for \( pqzwkfnb \). Thus the vertices of the pentagram \( xqvmsldp\ vlochwer\ myjvnxsr\ kuladqwe\ zasbqmnr \) must each be painted one of the two remaining colors. Then two adjacent vertices of the pentagram must have the same color. But they are separated by at least \( 2-\sqrt{2} \), so this is a contradiction.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ be an integer and put 
\[
S_{n}:=\Bigl\{(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})\in\mathbf R^{\,n}:\;
           x_{i}\ge 0\;(1\le i\le n),\;
           x_{1}+\dots +x_{n}\le 1\Bigr\},
\]
the right-isosceles $n$-simplex whose $n$ edges issuing from the origin
have Euclidean length $1$.  
Fix an integer $k\ge 1$ and colour every point of $S_{n}$ with one of
(at most) $k$ colours.  For a colouring~$\chi$ define
\[
d_{\max }(\chi):=\max_{\substack{x,y\in S_{n}\\
                         \chi(x)=\chi(y)}}\lVert x-y\rVert ,
\qquad
\Delta_{n,k}:=\inf_{\chi\text{ $k$-colouring of }S_{n}}d_{\max }(\chi).
\]

1. (Exact values for the ``few colours'' regime.)  
   Prove that, for every $n\ge 2$,
   \[
        \boxed{\;
        \Delta_{n,k}=
        \begin{cases}
        \sqrt{2}, & 1\le k\le n-1,\\[6pt]
        1,        & k=n.
        \end{cases}}
   \]
   Hence, a monochromatic pair of points at distance at least~$1$
   is unavoidable as long as fewer than $n+1$ colours are available.

2. (Uniformly positive gap for every finite number of colours.)  
   Put
   \[
      \omega_{n}:=\dfrac{\pi^{\,n/2}}{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac n2+1\bigr)},\qquad
      J_{n}:=\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{2(n+1)}}\quad(n\ge 2).
   \]
   Show that for every integer $k\ge n+1$
   \[
          \frac{1}{J_{n}\,\omega_n^{1/n}}
          \Bigl(\tfrac{1}{n!\,k}\Bigr)^{1/n}
          \;\le\;
          \Delta_{n,k}
          \;\le\;
          \sqrt n\,
          \frac{(n+1)^{1/n}}{k^{1/n}},
          \tag{$\ast$}
   \]
   and deduce that
   \[
      0<\liminf_{k\to\infty}k^{1/n}\,\Delta_{n,k}\le
      \limsup_{k\to\infty}k^{1/n}\,\Delta_{n,k}<\infty .
   \]
   Consequently, $\Delta_{n,k}>0$ for every finite $k$ and
   $\Delta_{n,k}= \Theta(k^{-1/n})$ as $k\to\infty$.

3. (Near-optimal colourings for $k=n$.)  
   In order to obtain a genuine \emph{partition} of $S_{n}$ we fix, once
   and for all, the following tie-breaking convention:  
   for $x\in S_{n}$ let $\max (x):=\max\{x_{1},\dots ,x_{n}\}$ and put
   \[
       \operatorname{dom}(x):=
       \min\bigl\{j:\,x_{j}=\max (x)\bigr\}.
   \]
   Define the $n$ dominant-coordinate regions
   \[
      \mathcal R_{j}:=\bigl\{x\in S_{n}:\;\operatorname{dom}(x)=j\bigr\},
      \qquad 1\le j\le n .
   \]

   (a)  Prove that
        \[
              \lVert x-y\rVert\le 1
              \qquad(x,y\in\mathcal R_{j},\;1\le j\le n),
        \]
        and conclude that the colouring
        $S_{n}=\bigsqcup_{j=1}^{n}\mathcal R_{j}$ satisfies
        $d_{\max }(\chi)=1$, hence attains $\Delta_{n,n}$.

   (b)  For every $\varepsilon>0$ construct an $n$-colouring
        $\chi_{\varepsilon}$ of $S_{n}$ such that
        $d_{\max }(\chi_{\varepsilon})\le 1+\varepsilon$.
        (Thus the bound $\Delta_{n,n}=1$ is ``stable''.)

Remark.  No claim is made that the diameter of every region
$\mathcal R_{j}$ equals $1$; in fact this is false whenever
$j>1$.  What matters is the uniform bound of part~(a).
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write
\[
\mathbf 0:=(0,\dots ,0),\qquad
e_{j}:=(0,\dots ,0,\underset{j}{1},0,\dots ,0)\;(1\le j\le n),\qquad
\mathcal V:=\{\mathbf 0,e_{1},\dots ,e_{n}\}.
\]
Because $S_{n}=\operatorname{conv}(\mathcal V)$, its diameter equals
$\sqrt{2}$.

\bigskip
\textbf{A counting lemma needed for part 2.}  
For an integer $m\ge 1$ subdivide the cube $[0,1]^{n}$ into
$m^{\,n}$ axis-parallel cubes of side-length $1/m$.  
Let $N(m)$ be the number of these small cubes that meet $S_{n}$.  
A cube is uniquely determined by its index vector
$I=(i_{1},\dots ,i_{n})$ with $0\le i_{t}\le m-1$, namely
\[
I\longmapsto\prod_{t=1}^{n}
      \Bigl[\tfrac{i_{t}}{m},\,\tfrac{i_{t}+1}{m}\Bigr].
\]
Such a cube intersects $S_{n}$ iff
\[
\frac{i_{1}+\dots +i_{n}}{m}\le 1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
i_{1}+\dots +i_{n}\le m-1 .
\]
Hence
\[
N(m)=\#\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{n})\in\mathbf Z_{\ge 0}^{\,n}:
              i_{1}+\dots +i_{n}\le m-1\Bigr\}
      =\binom{m-1+n}{n}.
\]

\emph{Two explicit upper bounds:}
\begin{align}
N(m)&=\binom{m-1+n}{n}\le(n+1)\,m^{\,n}\qquad(m\ge 1),\tag{1}\\
N(m)&=\binom{m-1+n}{n}\le(n+1)\,(m-1)^{\,n}\qquad(m\ge 2).\tag{2}
\end{align}

Inequality (1) is classical.  
For (2) put $R(m):=N(m)/(m-1)^{n}$ for $m\ge 2$ and observe
\[
\frac{R(m+1)}{R(m)}
   =\Bigl(1+\frac{n}{m}\Bigr)\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{m}\Bigr)^{n}\le 1,
\]
so $R(m)$ is decreasing and $R(m)\le R(2)=n+1$.

\bigskip
\textbf{1. Exact values for $k\le n$.}

(i) $1\le k\le n-1$.  
Among the $n$ vertices $e_{1},\dots ,e_{n}$ two share a colour, hence
$d_{\max }(\chi)\ge\sqrt{2}$.  
The constant colouring gives equality, so
$\Delta_{n,k}=\sqrt{2}$.

(ii) $k=n$.  
\emph{Lower bound.}
The $n+1$ points of $\mathcal V$ receive $n$ colours, so two share a
colour and are at distance at least $1$.  
Thus $\Delta_{n,n}\ge 1$.

\emph{Upper bound.}
Part~3 (a) below constructs an $n$-colouring with
$d_{\max }=1$, whence $\Delta_{n,n}=1$.

\bigskip
\textbf{2. Proof of $(\ast)$ for $k\ge n+1$.}

\emph{Lower bound (volume $+$ Jung).}  
Let $\chi$ be any $k$-colouring and put $\delta:=d_{\max }(\chi)$.  
By Jung's theorem every subset of diameter $\delta$ is contained in a
ball of radius $J_{n}\delta$, so every colour class has volume
$\le\omega_{n}(J_{n}\delta)^{n}$.  
Because $\operatorname{vol}(S_{n})=1/n!$,
\[
k\,\omega_{n}(J_{n}\delta)^{n}\ge\frac{1}{n!}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\delta\ge
\frac{1}{J_{n}\,\omega_{n}^{1/n}}
\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{n!\,k}\Bigr)^{1/n}.
\]

\emph{Upper bound (refined grid argument).}  
Set 
\[
m:=\max\Bigl\{2,\bigl\lceil(k/(n+1))^{1/n}\bigr\rceil\Bigr\}.
\]
By definition $m\ge 2$ and from (3) below we have
\[
(n+1)(m-1)^{\,n}<k\le(n+1)m^{\,n}.\tag{3}
\]
Together with~(2) this implies $N(m)<k$.  Colour each point of $S_{n}$
by the \emph{lexicographically} smallest cube (of side $1/m$) that
contains it.  
Two points of the same colour lie in one cube, whose diameter is
$\sqrt n/m$.  
Since $m\le(k/(n+1))^{1/n}+1$, the monotonicity of $x\mapsto1/x$
gives
\[
\frac{1}{m}\le\frac{(n+1)^{1/n}}{k^{1/n}},
\]
hence
\[
d_{\max }(\chi)\le\sqrt n\,\frac{(n+1)^{1/n}}{k^{1/n}},
\]
which is the right-hand side of~$(\ast)$.

\bigskip
\textbf{3. Near-optimal colourings for $k=n$.}

\medskip
\textbf{(a) The dominant-coordinate colouring attains $\Delta_{n,n}$.}

Recall
\[
\mathcal R_{j}:=\bigl\{x\in S_{n}:\;
          x_{j}>x_{i}\;(i<j),\;
          x_{j}\ge x_{i}\;(i>j)\bigr\},
\qquad
\chi(x):=\operatorname{dom}(x).
\]
Put
\[
\overline{\mathcal R_{j}}
 :=S_{n}\cap\bigl\{x\in\mathbf R^{\,n}:x_{j}\ge x_{i}\;\forall i\bigr\}.
\]
(The over-bar denotes the closure; the strict inequalities are removed.)
Observe that $\overline{\mathcal R_{j}}$ is the convex polytope  
\[
P_{j}:=\Bigl\{x\in\mathbf R^{\,n}:\;
            x_{i}\ge 0,\;
            \sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{i}\le 1,\;
            x_{j}\ge x_{i}\;\forall i\Bigr\}.
\]

\emph{Step 1: description of the extreme points of $P_{j}$.}  
For $k=0,\dots ,n$ set  
\[
v^{(k)}:=\frac1k\bigl(e_{j}+e_{i_{1}}+\dots +e_{i_{k-1}}\bigr)
\quad(k\ge 1),\qquad
v^{(0)}:=\mathbf 0,
\]
where $\{i_{1},\dots ,i_{k-1}\}\subset\{1,\dots ,n\}\setminus\{j\}$.
Every $v^{(k)}$ lies in $P_{j}$, and a routine linear-programming
argument shows that
\[
\operatorname{Ext}(P_{j})=\bigl\{v^{(k)}:0\le k\le n\bigr\}.
\]

\emph{Step 2: pairwise distances between extreme points.}
\[
\lVert v^{(0)}-e_{j}\rVert=1,\quad
\lVert v^{(0)}-v^{(k)}\rVert=\sqrt{\tfrac1k}<1\;(k\ge 2),\quad
\lVert e_{j}-v^{(k)}\rVert
   =\sqrt{1-\tfrac1k}<1\;(k\ge 2),
\]
and, by the triangle inequality, every other pair of extreme points is
also at distance $<1$.

\emph{Step 3: diameter of $P_{j}$ and of $\mathcal R_{j}$.}  
The squared Euclidean distance
\[
D(x,y):=\lVert x-y\rVert^{2}
\]
is jointly convex in $(x,y)$.  Over a compact convex set a convex
function attains its maximum at an extreme point, whence
\[
\max_{(x,y)\in P_{j}\times P_{j}}D(x,y)
      =\max_{u,v\in\operatorname{Ext}(P_{j})}D(u,v)=1,
\]
the maximum being attained (uniquely) by the pair $(\mathbf 0,e_{j})$.
Because $\mathcal R_{j}\subset P_{j}$ we conclude that
\[
\lVert x-y\rVert\le 1
\qquad(x,y\in\mathcal R_{j}),
\]
and equality is indeed possible (take $x=\mathbf 0$, $y=e_{j}$).
Consequently $d_{\max }(\chi)=1$, so the dominant-coordinate
colouring realises $\Delta_{n,n}$.

\medskip
\textbf{(b) Approximate sharpness (stability).}

Let $\varepsilon>0$ be given and put
\[
\gamma:=\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sqrt{2}},\qquad
\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}:=\Bigl\{x\in\mathcal R_{j}:\;
x_{j}\ge x_{i}+\gamma\;\forall i\neq j\Bigr\}.
\]
The point $e_{j}$ satisfies the above inequalities, hence every
$\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$ is non-empty.
Moreover $\operatorname{diam}(\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma})\le 1$
because $\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}\subset\mathcal R_{j}$.

Define the colouring
\[
\chi_{\varepsilon}(x):=
\begin{cases}
j, & x\in\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma},\\[4pt]
\operatorname{dom}(x), & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]

\emph{Bringing a point close to $\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$.}
Fix $x\notin\bigcup_{i}\mathcal R_{i}^{\gamma}$ and set
$j=\operatorname{dom}(x)$.
Since $x\notin\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$ we have
$d_{\min}:=\min_{i\neq j}(x_{j}-x_{i})<\gamma$.  
Define 
\[
x':=(1-\gamma)\,x+\gamma\,e_{j}.
\]
Because $x,e_{j}\in S_{n}$, their convex combination $x'$ also lies in
$S_{n}$.
Furthermore
\[
x'_{j}-x'_{i}=(1-\gamma)(x_{j}-x_{i})+\gamma
              \ge (1-\gamma)d_{\min}+\gamma\ge\gamma
\quad(i\neq j),
\]
so $x'\in\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$.
The displacement is small:
\[
\lVert x-x'\rVert
     =\gamma\,\lVert x-e_{j}\rVert
     \le\gamma\,\sqrt{2}
     =\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]

\emph{Bounding the monochromatic diameter.}
Take $x,y$ with $\chi_{\varepsilon}(x)=\chi_{\varepsilon}(y)=j$.
If both $x,y$ already lie in $\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$, then
$\lVert x-y\rVert\le 1$.
Otherwise replace the offending points by $x',y'$ obtained through the
above procedure; these satisfy $x',y'\in\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$ and
\[
\lVert x-y\rVert
  \le\lVert x-x'\rVert+\lVert x'-y'\rVert+\lVert y'-y\rVert
  \le\frac{\varepsilon}{2}+1+\frac{\varepsilon}{2}
  =1+\varepsilon .
\]
Thus $d_{\max }(\chi_{\varepsilon})\le 1+\varepsilon$, completing the
proof of stability.

\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension:  the problem is now set in $\mathbb R^{\,n}$ for arbitrary $n\ge 2$, introducing $n$ variables instead of 2.  
2. More colours and tighter bound:  precisely $n$ colours are allowed and an explicit dimension–dependent bound $d_n$ must be proved and shown optimal.  
3. Additional tasks:  besides proving existence of a distant monochromatic pair, the solver must also construct an \emph{almost extremal colouring}, establishing sharpness.  
4. Deeper techniques:  the solution demands (i) a cleverly chosen $(n+1)$-point configuration with controlled pairwise distances, (ii) careful distance estimates in $\mathbb R^{\,n}$, (iii) a geometric pigeon-hole argument, and (iv) a two-part constructive sharpness proof involving both “corner caps’’ and fine barycentric tilings.  

These layers of abstraction and computation go well beyond the planar, single-bound, existence-only argument required in both the original and the current kernel variants, making the enhanced problem significantly more challenging.

S_{n}:=\Bigl\{(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})\in\mathbf R^{\,n}:\;

\]

   \]

      \qquad 1\le j\le n .
   \]

   (a)  Prove that
        \[
              \lVert x-y\rVert\le 1
              \qquad(x,y\in\mathcal R_{j},\;1\le j\le n),
        \]
        and conclude that the colouring
        $S_{n}=\bigsqcup_{j=1}^{n}\mathcal R_{j}$ satisfies
        $d_{\max }(\chi)=1$, hence attains $\Delta_{n,n}$.

   (b)  For every $\varepsilon>0$ construct an $n$-colouring
        $\chi_{\varepsilon}$ of $S_{n}$ such that
        $d_{\max }(\chi_{\varepsilon})\le 1+\varepsilon$.
        (Thus the bound $\Delta_{n,n}=1$ is ``stable''.)

Remark.  No claim is made that the diameter of every region
$\mathcal R_{j}$ equals $1$; in fact this is false whenever
$j>1$.  What matters is the uniform bound of part (a).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write
\[
\mathbf 0:=(0,\dots ,0),\qquad
e_{j}:=(0,\dots ,0,\underset{j}{1},0,\dots ,0)\;(1\le j\le n),\qquad
\mathcal V:=\{\mathbf 0,e_{1},\dots ,e_{n}\}.
\]
Because $S_{n}=\operatorname{conv}(\mathcal V)$, its diameter equals
$\sqrt{2}$.

\bigskip
\textbf{A counting lemma needed for part 2.}  \\
For an integer $m\ge 1$ subdivide the cube $[0,1]^{n}$ into
$m^{\,n}$ axis-parallel cubes of side-length $1/m$.  
Let $N(m)$ be the number of these small cubes that meet $S_{n}$.  
A cube is uniquely determined by its index vector
$I=(i_{1},\dots ,i_{n})$ with $0\le i_{t}\le m-1$, namely
\[
I\longmapsto\prod_{t=1}^{n}
      \Bigl[\tfrac{i_{t}}{m},\,\tfrac{i_{t}+1}{m}\Bigr].
\]
Such a cube intersects $S_{n}$ iff
\[
\frac{i_{1}+\dots +i_{n}}{m}\le 1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
i_{1}+\dots +i_{n}\le m-1 .
\]
Hence
\[
N(m)=\#\Bigl\{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{n})\in\mathbf Z_{\ge 0}^{\,n}:
              i_{1}+\dots +i_{n}\le m-1\Bigr\}
      =\binom{m-1+n}{n}.
\]

\emph{Two explicit upper bounds:}
\begin{align}
N(m)&=\binom{m-1+n}{n}\le(n+1)\,m^{\,n}\qquad(m\ge 1),\tag{1}\\
N(m)&=\binom{m-1+n}{n}\le(n+1)\,(m-1)^{\,n}\qquad(m\ge 2).\tag{2}
\end{align}

Inequality (1) is classical.  
For (2) put $R(m):=N(m)/(m-1)^{n}$ for $m\ge 2$ and observe
\[
\frac{R(m+1)}{R(m)}
   =\Bigl(1+\frac{n}{m}\Bigr)\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{m}\Bigr)^{n}\le 1,
\]
so $R(m)$ is decreasing and $R(m)\le R(2)=n+1$.

\bigskip
\textbf{1. Exact values for $k\le n$.}

(i) $1\le k\le n-1$.  
Among the $n$ vertices $e_{1},\dots ,e_{n}$ two share a colour, hence
$d_{\max }(\chi)\ge\sqrt{2}$.  
The constant colouring gives equality, so
$\Delta_{n,k}=\sqrt{2}$.

(ii) $k=n$.  
\emph{Lower bound.}
The $n+1$ points of $\mathcal V$ receive $n$ colours, so two share a
colour and are at distance at least $1$.  
Thus $\Delta_{n,n}\ge 1$.

\emph{Upper bound.}
Part 3 (a) below constructs an $n$-colouring with
$d_{\max }=1$, whence $\Delta_{n,n}=1$.

\bigskip
\textbf{2. Proof of $(\ast)$ for $k\ge n+1$.}

\emph{Lower bound (volume $+$ Jung).}  
Let $\chi$ be any $k$-colouring and put $\delta:=d_{\max }(\chi)$.  
By Jung's theorem every subset of diameter $\delta$ is contained in a
ball of radius $J_{n}\delta$, so every colour class has volume
$\le\omega_{n}(J_{n}\delta)^{n}$.  
Because $\operatorname{vol}(S_{n})=1/n!$,
\[
k\,\omega_{n}(J_{n}\delta)^{n}\ge\frac{1}{n!}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\delta\ge
\frac{1}{J_{n}\,\omega_{n}^{1/n}}
\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{n!\,k}\Bigr)^{1/n}.
\]

\emph{Upper bound (refined grid argument).}  
Set 
\[
m:=\max\Bigl\{2,\bigl\lceil(k/(n+1))^{1/n}\bigr\rceil\Bigr\}.
\]
By definition $m\ge 2$ and from (3) below we have
\[
(n+1)(m-1)^{\,n}<k\le(n+1)m^{\,n}.\tag{3}
\]
Together with (2) this implies $N(m)<k$.  Colour each point of $S_{n}$
by the \emph{lexicographically} smallest cube (of side $1/m$) that
contains it.  
Two points of the same colour lie in one cube, whose diameter is
$\sqrt n/m$.  
Since $m\le(k/(n+1))^{1/n}+1$, the monotonicity of $x\mapsto1/x$
gives
\[
\frac{1}{m}\le\frac{(n+1)^{1/n}}{k^{1/n}},
\]
hence
\[
d_{\max }(\chi)\le\sqrt n\,\frac{(n+1)^{1/n}}{k^{1/n}},
\]
which is the right-hand side of $(\ast)$.

\bigskip
\textbf{3. Near-optimal colourings for $k=n$.}

\medskip
\textbf{(a) The dominant-coordinate colouring attains $\Delta_{n,n}$.}

Recall
\[
\mathcal R_{j}:=\bigl\{x\in S_{n}:\;
          x_{j}>x_{i}\;(i<j),\;
          x_{j}\ge x_{i}\;(i>j)\bigr\},
\qquad
\chi(x):=\operatorname{dom}(x).
\]
Put
\[
\overline{\mathcal R_{j}}
 :=S_{n}\cap\bigl\{x\in\mathbf R^{\,n}:x_{j}\ge x_{i}\;\forall i\bigr\}.
\]
(The over-bar denotes the closure; the strict inequalities are removed.)
Observe that $\overline{\mathcal R_{j}}$ is the convex polytope  
\[
P_{j}:=\Bigl\{x\in\mathbf R^{\,n}:\;
            x_{i}\ge 0,\;
            \sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{i}\le 1,\;
            x_{j}\ge x_{i}\;\forall i\Bigr\}.
\]

\emph{Step 1: description of the extreme points of $P_{j}$.}  
For $k=0,\dots ,n$ set  
\[
v^{(k)}:=\frac1k\bigl(e_{j}+e_{i_{1}}+\dots +e_{i_{k-1}}\bigr)
\quad(k\ge 1),\qquad
v^{(0)}:=\mathbf 0,
\]
where $\{i_{1},\dots ,i_{k-1}\}\subset\{1,\dots ,n\}\setminus\{j\}$.
Every $v^{(k)}$ lies in $P_{j}$, and a routine linear-programming
argument (or an enumeration of tight constraints) shows that
\[
\operatorname{Ext}(P_{j})=\bigl\{v^{(k)}:0\le k\le n\bigr\}.
\]

\emph{Step 2: pairwise distances between extreme points.}
\[
\lVert v^{(0)}-e_{j}\rVert=1,
\qquad
\lVert v^{(0)}-v^{(k)}\rVert=\sqrt{\frac1k}<1\;(k\ge 2),
\qquad
\lVert e_{j}-v^{(k)}\rVert
   =\sqrt{1-\frac1k}<1\;(k\ge 2),
\]
and, by the triangle inequality, every other pair of extreme points is
also at distance $<1$.

\emph{Step 3: diameter of $P_{j}$ and of $\mathcal R_{j}$.}  
The squared Euclidean distance
\[
D(x,y):=\lVert x-y\rVert^{2}
\]
is jointly convex in $(x,y)$.  Over a compact convex set a convex
function attains its maximum at an extreme point, whence
\[
\max_{(x,y)\in P_{j}\times P_{j}}D(x,y)
      =\max_{u,v\in\operatorname{Ext}(P_{j})}D(u,v)=1,
\]
the maximum being attained (uniquely) by the pair $(\mathbf 0,e_{j})$.
Because
$\mathcal R_{j}\subset P_{j}$, we conclude that
\[
\lVert x-y\rVert\le 1
\qquad(x,y\in\mathcal R_{j}),
\]
and equality is indeed possible (take $x=\mathbf 0$, $y=e_{j}$).
Consequently $d_{\max }(\chi)=1$, so the dominant-coordinate
colouring realises $\Delta_{n,n}$.

\medskip
\textbf{(b) Approximate sharpness (stability).}

Let $0<\varepsilon<1$ and put
\[
\gamma:=\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sqrt n},\qquad
\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}:=\Bigl\{x\in\mathcal R_{j}:\;
x_{j}\ge x_{i}+\gamma\;\forall i\neq j\Bigr\}.
\]
Because the set $\{x_{j}-x_{i}\mid x\in\mathcal R_{j}\}$ contains
$(0,1]$ and $\gamma<1$, each $\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$ is non-empty and
$\operatorname{diam}(\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma})\le 1$
(as it is a subset of $\mathcal R_{j}$).

Define the colouring
\[
\chi_{\varepsilon}(x):=
\begin{cases}
j, & x\in\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma},\\[4pt]
\operatorname{dom}(x), & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
For $x$ outside every $\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$ let
$x':=(x+\gamma e_{j})/(1+\gamma)$ with 
$j=\operatorname{dom}(x)$; then $x'\in\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$ and
$\lVert x'-x\rVert\le\gamma\sqrt n=\varepsilon/2$.  

If $x,y$ share colour $j$, either both lie in $\mathcal R_{j}^{\gamma}$
and $\lVert x-y\rVert\le 1$, or we replace the offending points by
$x',y'$ and obtain
\[
\lVert x-y\rVert
     \le\lVert x-x'\rVert+\lVert x'-y'\rVert+\lVert y'-y\rVert
     \le\frac{\varepsilon}{2}+1+\frac{\varepsilon}{2}=1+\varepsilon .
\]
Thus $d_{\max }(\chi_{\varepsilon})\le 1+\varepsilon$, proving stability.

\hfill$\square$

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension:  the problem is now set in $\mathbb R^{\,n}$ for arbitrary $n\ge 2$, introducing $n$ variables instead of 2.  
2. More colours and tighter bound:  precisely $n$ colours are allowed and an explicit dimension–dependent bound $d_n$ must be proved and shown optimal.  
3. Additional tasks:  besides proving existence of a distant monochromatic pair, the solver must also construct an \emph{almost extremal colouring}, establishing sharpness.  
4. Deeper techniques:  the solution demands (i) a cleverly chosen $(n+1)$-point configuration with controlled pairwise distances, (ii) careful distance estimates in $\mathbb R^{\,n}$, (iii) a geometric pigeon-hole argument, and (iv) a two-part constructive sharpness proof involving both “corner caps’’ and fine barycentric tilings.  

These layers of abstraction and computation go well beyond the planar, single-bound, existence-only argument required in both the original and the current kernel variants, making the enhanced problem significantly more challenging.
